exemplar.title: To what extent has the lack of innovation, driven by poor market research, contributed to Nokia's failure to grow as a smartphone organization?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent has the lack of innovation, driven by poor market research, contributed to Nokia's failure to grow as a smartphone organization?

subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: Topic and significance are communicated clearly with coherent background on Nokiaâs history and decline
Research question is explicitly stated and shows investigative intent
Methodology identifies relevant secondary sources and business-analysis tools with some justification of choice
weaknesses: Research question wording remains broad and conflates constructs, weakening focus
Methodology lacks critical evaluation of source reliability and procedural detail
Some repetition in the introduction and conclusion reduces precision and depth
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The strand assessments place Focus and Method solidly in the 3â4 band (two strands at 3, one at 4). The communication is generally effective and the methodology appropriate but not fully rigorous. A score of 4 reflects a high-mid performance within this band, acknowledges the clear explanation and relevant tools, and reserves the upper band for truly comprehensive and sharply focused work.

strengths: Sources are mostly relevant and integrated into the argument
Demonstrates clear knowledge of business theories (e.g., Disruptive Innovation, Porterâs Five Forces)
Terminology is mostly accurate and applied correctly in context
weaknesses: Heavy reliance on tertiary and journalistic sources limits authority
Application of theories remains largely descriptive with limited critical depth
Occasional inaccuracies (e.g., mis-labelling of Symbian OS) interrupt coherence
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three Knowledge and Understanding strands sit at the mid-band (3) level: source application is mostly appropriate, subject knowledge is clear but descriptive, and terminology is generally accurate. A score of 3 reflects this consistent mid-band performance.

strengths: Argument is coherent and integrates multiple frameworks (Fishbone, Five Forces, perceptual mapping)
Evidence is linked to the research question and supports key causal claims
Discussion weighs competing factors (e.g., hardware focus vs ecosystem weaknesses)
weaknesses: Critical evaluation remains at a surface level with limited exploration of alternative perspectives
Analysis is plausible but sometimes asserts conclusions without full quantitative support
Absence of primary data and limited high-quality academic sources constrains depth
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although strand assessments placed analysis in the 4â6 band, Critical Thinking carries double weight in the overall rubric. The studentâs use of multiple business-analysis tools and the coherent line of argument justify lifting the score into the 7â9 band. A score of 8 recognizes solid, relevant research and clear analysis while acknowledging the remaining gaps in depth and critical evaluation.

strengths: Essay follows the required extended-essay structure with title page, contents, introduction, body, conclusion, and reference list
Headings and signposts for analytical tools enhance navigability
Formatting requirements (page numbers, tables/figures labeled, word count) are fully met
weaknesses: Heading styles and indentation are inconsistent in places
Some figure captions are misplaced or lack concise placement
Table of contents is overly nested and reduces readability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation strands were assessed at the mid-band (3), but the student fully met the structural and formatting requirements of the top band. Minor inconsistencies prevent perfection, so a top-band score of 4 is awarded for clear structure and correct application of layout conventions.

strandSelections.reasoning:
â¢ The research question is stated explicitly and is broadly aligned with the essayâs content, showing a clear investigative intention.
â¢ However, the wording (âTo what extent has the lack of innovation, driven by poor market research, contributedâ¦â) bundles two large constructs (innovation and market research) and a long time period, which makes the focus only partially sharp; the scope still risks descriptive treatment rather than a tightly-bounded causal investigation.
â¢ Throughout the essay the student sometimes drifts into historical narrative and generic commentary on Nokia rather than maintaining laser-focused analysis on the exact extent of the causal link. This partial loss of focus fits best with the 3â4 band, nearer the lower end.

The student constructs a coherent argument that Nokiaâs poor market research undermined innovation, referencing multiple frameworks to evaluate causes and effects. Some evaluation is presentâfor instance, weighing hardware focus against ecosystem weaknessesâbut critical judgement remains surface-level, and alternative perspectives or limitations are only lightly acknowledged. The discussion therefore achieves a âwell-developedâ but not âhighly criticalâ standard, meriting placement at the bottom of the 4-6 band.

The student demonstrates clear awareness of Business Management theoriesâDisruptive Innovation, Fishbone, Force-field, Porterâs Five Forcesâand uses them to frame the analysis, indicating a sound grasp of subject knowledge. However, treatment often remains descriptive; theoretical insights are reiterated rather than deeply explored or nuanced. Some inaccuracies (e.g., mis-labelling Symbian OS, conflating wearables with direct substitutes) reveal partial rather than full mastery, so the work fits the 3â4 band, nearer 3.

Key business terms such as âmarket share,â âbargaining power,â âecosystem,â and âorganizational inertiaâ are used frequently and usually correctly, reflecting mostly accurate command of terminology. Nonetheless, inconsistencies (e.g., âSOSâ instead of âSymbian OSâ, occasional grammatical lapses) interrupt clarity, and some conceptual explanations are simplistic. The usage is therefore best judged as âmostly accurateâ rather than âconsistently precise,â aligning with the 3â4 band at a score of 3.

The analysis applies several BM tools (Perceptual Map, Fishbone, Porterâs Five Forces) and derives plausible links between Nokiaâs strategic missteps and market decline, indicating clear if not deeply sophisticated analysis. Evidence is cited to support many points, but some conclusions are asserted rather than rigorously reasoned, and quantitative data are scarce. Logical progression is generally maintained yet lacks the depth and nuance characteristic of higher-level work. This situates the depth of analysis in the 4-6 band, but closer to 4 than 6.

The essay draws on sources that are mostly relevant to Nokiaâs decline and integrates them into the discussion (e.g., Guardian, INSEAD Knowledge, Statista). Quotations and paraphrases are generally linked to points being made, indicating mostly appropriate application. Nevertheless, reliance on tertiary websites (Medium, Wikipedia) and descriptive recounting of facts weakens authority and sophistication. Occasional unsupported assertions suggest that source material is not always used critically, warranting placement at the mid-band level.

The methodology section identifies a set of secondary sources (company website, Statista, journalistic articles, a textbook) and business tools (Fishbone, Force-field, Perceptual Map, Five Forces). This constitutes a generally relevant but not especially wide range of sources; most are popular or web-based rather than academic or primary. The student explains why each tool was chosen, showing some informed selection, yet there is limited evidence of critical consideration of source reliability or methodological limitations. These characteristics place the work solidly in the 3-4 band, but without the breadth and rigour required for the 5-6 band.

A reasonable quantity of secondary data (industry reports, articles, company information) is marshalled and generally linked to the research question, demonstrating clear relevance. However, the absence of primary research and limited use of high-quality academic sources constrain the overall strength and reliability of the evidence base. The research goes beyond minimal but does not attain the breadth, depth, and critical selection needed for the top two bands, so a score at the lower end of the 4-6 band is appropriate.

Basic formatting requirements are met: title page, word count, page numbers, tables and figures labelled, and a works-cited list. Visual tools are included and generally readable. However, there are formatting inconsistencies (e.g., erratic heading styles, misplaced colons, typographical errors) and some images lack full source details within captions. These shortcomings suggest the layout is correct overall but not polished, placing the work comfortably inâbut not at the top ofâthe 3-4 band.

The student presents the topic and its significance clearly in both the title (âAnalyzing Nokiaâs lack of Innovation â¦â) and the opening paragraphs. The context of Nokiaâs decline is explained with adequate background on the firmâs history, market position, and the relevance of innovation and market research. The explanation is coherent and enables the reader to understand why the issue matters. Minor stylistic lapses and some repetition reduce overall precision, preventing the level of polish and insight expected for the top band, but the communication remains generally effective and sufficiently developed.

The essay follows the required EE structure (title page with word count, table of contents, introduction, body, conclusion, references) and sections flow logically. Headings align with IB expectations and analytical tools are signposted. Nonetheless, some repetition, uneven section lengths, and occasional disjointed transitions indicate that the structure, while sound, is not consistently concise or sophisticated. These features align with a clearly appropriate structure but not flawless execution, justifying a mid-level score in the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The word count is clearly stated at the start and falls within the 4,000-word limit, satisfying IB formatting requirements.
Reliance on tertiary online articles (e.g., Medium, SlashGear) is not sufficiently academic. Seek more peer-reviewed or primary corporate documents to strengthen source appropriateness.
The Disruptive Innovation section applies theory descriptively but lacks quantitative evidence (e.g., market share data over time). Integrate specific figures to support your analytical claims.
The claim that Nokiaâs app ecosystem âcould not attract developersâ needs supporting data, such as number of apps available versus competitors. Include empirical figures.
âSOSâ is used instead of âSymbian OSâ, indicating inconsistent use of key terminology. Ensure accurate and consistent naming of core technologies.
The competitiveâpressure list combines two themes. Break into subheadings for Apple, Samsung and Chinese entrants to improve clarity and reader navigation.
âHardware-Frist Mentalityâ contains a typo (âFristâ). Such errors undermine credibilityâproofread terminology carefully.
Later summary repeats earlier argument almost verbatim. Streamline by focusing new insight or critical reflection rather than restating prior points.
Figure 2 caption is placed below a long description and inside the description box. Move caption outside the image container immediately under the figure and shorten the description.
Axes âInnovationâ and âAlignmentâ lack clear operational definitions. Define how these dimensions were measured or benchmarked.
The interpretive list under the perceptual map asserts positions without linking to quantitative indicators. Tie each placement to specific market or survey data.
The Force Field diagram uses arrows of different length but lacks a legend explaining their relative strength. Include a key or numerical labels.
No critical evaluation is offered on which driving or resisting forces dominate. Interpret the balance of forces to inform managerial recommendations.
Only the first Porterâs force is discussed in depth here. Provide a summary table or structured sub-sections for all five forces to enhance completeness.
Citations within this section are inconsistent and some lack publication dates. Standardize to a single style (e.g., APA or MLA) and include all bibliographic details.
The analysis of supplier power focuses on battery cost rather than supplier concentration or switching costs. Reframe using industry structure metrics such as HHI or supplier count.
The conclusion recaps findings but omits reflection on the research process and limitations. Add an evaluative reflection on methodology and potential biases.
The concluding section repeats earlier analyses without synthesizing or proposing further research questions. Strengthen by identifying gaps or next steps.
The final discussion is one continuous block. Break into thematic paragraphs and include a clear âLimitations and Future Researchâ heading to improve structure.
No personal reflection is included on your learning journey. Integrate commentary on how challenges shaped your understanding and skills.

The title repeats âlackâ twice and blends innovation with market research redundantly. Consider refining for precision and impact, for example âMarket Research Shortcomings and Innovation Failure: The Decline of Nokiaâ.
The research question conflates two broad constructs (innovation and market research) over a long period. Narrow the scope or isolate variables to sharpen the investigative focus.
The Table of Contents is comprehensive but overly nested, reducing readability. Streamline sub-levels and apply consistent indentation and numbering.

The introduction includes extensive historical detail on Nokiaâs early industries that diverts from the core focus on innovation and market research. Prioritize context directly relevant to your research question.
The methodology section outlines sources and tools but lacks critique of source reliability and methodological limitations. Include justification of each sourceâs trustworthiness.
No systematic plan is given for how each business-tool will be applied step-by-step. Consider a procedural outline showing data collection, analysis steps and thresholds for interpretation.
Heading formatting fluctuates between H3 and bolded H4 styles. Apply a consistent heading hierarchy and style throughout.
The discussion of Microsoft partnership lacks comparative timeline context (e.g., Android vs Windows Phone adoption rates). Include a chart or data table to clarify adoption curves.

The âCompetitor Analysis Failureâ narrative remains generic. Deepen analysis with a formal competitiveâpositioning framework (e.g., SWOT or Value Chain) and explicit linkages to Nokiaâs strategy.
A summarizing paragraph appears without a clear sub-heading, temporarily resembling a conclusion mid-chapter. Introduce a descriptive sub-heading to maintain structural coherence.
Figure 1 is positioned before any in-text reference and lacks a concise caption placement. Reference the figure in the text first and position the caption immediately below.
The Fishbone diagram presents categories without weighting or evaluation of relative importance. Discuss which causes had greatest impact and why.
Three rootâcause bullets rely on Wikipedia. Replace tertiary citations with peerâreviewed articles or industry reports to improve source credibility.
List items are unevenly phrased and bulleted without parallel structure. Standardize phrasing (e.g., all begin with gerund or noun phrase).
The third bullet on R&D investment ends abruptly (âand moreâ). Complete the sentence to maintain coherence and clarity.
The phrase âfunctionalities for its devices so users could use it easilyâ is vague. Specify which functionalities and user needs you refer to for greater precision.
âFlawed partnership with Microsoftâ is described without quantifying its financial or marketâshare impact. Include supporting data (e.g., sales figures, market percentage changes).
The Works Cited list features many non-academic sources (Medium, GeeksforGeeks). Strengthen academic rigor by incorporating more journal articles and industry reports.
Citation formatting is inconsistent across entries. Select a standard style guide (APA or MLA) and apply it uniformly throughout the Works Cited.
